📄 A Survey on Retrieval-Augmented Generation.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of Visual**: Flowchart 2. **Title or Heading**: Not explicitly titled, but the main theme is "Retrieval—Augmented Generation." 3. **Axis Labels, Units, and Scale Ranges**: - Vertical timeline on the left side with years marked: 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024. - Key events: "GPT-3 2020.6" and "ChatGPT 2022.12." 4. **Data Series Names and Key Points**: - **Pre-training**: Includes "InstructRetro," "Raven," "GraphToolformer," "TOC," "Retro++," "COG," "Atlas," "RETRO," "REALM." - **Fine-tuning**: Includes "SUGRE," "Self-RAG," "RA-DIT," "AAR," "REPLUG," "Selfmem," "SANTA," "UPRISE," "FiD," "RAG," "DPR." - **Inference**: Includes "GenRead," "CREA-ICL," "IRCOT," "COK," "PKG," "KAR," "DSP," "PRCA," "KnowledgeGPT," "KNN-LLM," "RECITE." 5. **Text Visible in the Image**: - **Augmentation Stage**: "Pre-training," "Fine-tuning," "Inference." - **Augmentation Data**: "Unstructured Data," "Structured Data," "LLM Generated Content." - **Legend**: - Green for "Unstructured Data" - Yellow for "Structured Data" - Orange for "LLM Generated Content" - "ChatGPT 2022.12" and "GPT-3 2020.6" are marked on the timeline. 6. **Specific Numbers, Percentages, Dates, Currency Values, or Named Entities**: - Dates: 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024. - Named entities: "ChatGPT," "GPT-3." 7. **Overall Trend, Conclusion, or Key Insight**: - The flowchart illustrates the evolution and development stages of retrieval-augmented generation models over time, categorized into pre-training, fine-tuning, and inference stages. It shows the progression and branching of various models and techniques from 2020 to 2024. 8. **Components, Their Relationships, and Directional Flow**: - The flowchart is structured like a tree with roots in "Retrieval—Augmented Generation," branching into three main categories: "Pre-training," "Fine-tuning," and "Inference." - Each category further branches into specific models or techniques, indicating their development and interconnections over time. This detailed description provides a comprehensive understanding of the flowchart, its components, and the relationships between different models and techniques in the context of retrieval-augmented generation.

📄 A Survey on Retrieval-Augmented Generation.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of Visual**: Conceptual diagram 2. **Title or Heading**: None visible 3. **Axis Labels**: - Vertical Axis: "External Knowledge Required" with a range from "Low" at the bottom to "High" at the top. - Horizontal Axis: "Model Adaptation Required" with a range from "Low" on the left to "High" on the right. 4. **Data Series Names and Key Points**: - **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)**: Positioned centrally, indicating a balance between external knowledge and model adaptation. - **Fine-tuning**: Positioned towards the right, indicating high model adaptation required. - **Prompt Engineering**: Positioned towards the bottom left, indicating low external knowledge and model adaptation required. - **All of the above**: Positioned centrally, overlapping RAG and Fine-tuning. 5. **Text Visible in the Image**: - **Modular RAG**: "Organic combination of multiple modules" (Top left, high external knowledge, moderate model adaptation) - **Advanced RAG**: "Index/pre-retrieval/post-retrieval optimization" (Above Naive RAG, moderate external knowledge) - **Naive RAG**: "Add relevant contextual paragraphs" (Below Advanced RAG, low external knowledge) - **Adding few shot case COT**: (Below Naive RAG, low external knowledge and model adaptation) - **Prompt preliminary attempts**: (Bottom left, low external knowledge and model adaptation) - **Retriever fine-tuning**: (Top right, high model adaptation) - **Collaborative fine-tuning**: (Right, high model adaptation) - **Generator fine-tuning**: (Bottom right, high model adaptation) 6. **Specific Numbers, Percentages, Dates, Currency Values, or Named Entities**: None 7. **Overall Trend, Conclusion, or Key Insight**: - The diagram illustrates the relationship between external knowledge required and model adaptation required for different approaches in AI model development, specifically focusing on RAG, fine-tuning, and prompt engineering. It suggests that RAG and fine-tuning require more model adaptation, while prompt engineering requires less external knowledge and adaptation. 8. **Components, Relationships, and Directional Flow**: - The diagram shows a progression from "Prompt preliminary attempts" to "Adding few shot case COT" to "Naive RAG" to "Advanced RAG" to "Modular RAG" as external knowledge increases. - Fine-tuning components (Retriever, Collaborative, Generator) are aligned horizontally, indicating varying levels of model adaptation. - Arrows indicate potential pathways and relationships between different components, suggesting a flow from simpler to more complex methods. This detailed description captures all visible elements and their relationships within the diagram.

📄 A Survey on Retrieval-Augmented Generation.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of Visual**: Architecture diagram 2. **Title or Heading**: Not explicitly titled, but the diagram is divided into three sections: "Naive RAG," "Advanced RAG," and "Modular RAG." 3. **Axis Labels, Units, and Scale Ranges**: Not applicable. 4. **Data Series Names and Values**: Not applicable. 5. **Text Visible in the Image**: - **Naive RAG**: - User - Query - Documents - Document Chunks - Vector Database - Related Document Chunks - Prompt - LLM - **Advanced RAG**: - User - Query - Documents - Document Chunks - Vector Database - Related Document Chunks - Pre-Retrieval - Fine-grained Data Cleaning - Sliding Window / Small2Big - Add File Structure - Query Rewrite/Clarification - Retriever Router - Post-Retrieval - Rerank - Filter - Prompt Compression - Prompt - LLM - **Modular RAG**: - New Modules: - Search - Criticize - Predict - Retrieve - Rerank - Rewrite - Read - Demonstrate - Reflect - Filter - New Patterns: - Rewrite → Retrieve → Read (RRR [Ma et al., 2023]) - Demonstrate → Search → Predict (DSP [Khattab et al., 2022]) - Retrieve → Read → Retrieve → Read (ITER-RETGEN [Shao et al., 2023]) - Decide → Retrieve → Generate → Criticize (Self-RAG [Asai et al., 2023b]) 6. **Specific Numbers, Percentages, Dates, Currency Values, or Named Entities**: - References to academic papers: Ma et al., 2023; Khattab et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2023; Asai et al., 2023b. 7. **Overall Trend, Conclusion, or Key Insight**: - The diagram illustrates the evolution of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems from a basic "Naive RAG" to a more sophisticated "Advanced RAG" with pre-retrieval and post-retrieval enhancements, and finally to a "Modular RAG" with new modules and patterns for improved document retrieval and processing. 8. **Tables**: Not applicable. 9. **Diagram Components, Relationships, and Directional Flow**: - **Naive RAG**: - Flow from User and Query to Documents, then to Document Chunks, Vector Database, and Related Document Chunks, leading to Prompt and LLM. - **Advanced RAG**: - Similar flow as Naive RAG with additional Pre-Retrieval and Post-Retrieval processes enhancing the retrieval and processing stages. - **Modular RAG**: - Central RAG module with interconnected new modules (Search, Criticize, Predict, Retrieve, Rerank, Rewrite, Read, Demonstrate, Reflect, Filter). - New patterns show different sequences of module interactions for specific retrieval and generation tasks.

📄 A Survey on Retrieval-Augmented Generation.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of Visual**: Flowchart 2. **Title or Heading**: Retrieval-Augmented Generation 3. **Text and Labels**: - **Main Branches**: - Retriever (§4) - Generator (§5) - Augmentation Method (§6) - **Retriever Sub-branches**: - Better Semantic Representation - Chunk Optimization - Fine-tuning Embedding Model - Align Queries and Documents - Query Rewrite/Clarification - Embedding Transformation - Align Retriever and LLM - Plugin Adapter - LLM Supervised Training - **Generator Sub-branches**: - Post-retrieval with Frozen LLM - Information Compression - Rerank - General Optimization Process - Utilizing Contrastive Learning - Fine-tuning LLM for RAG - **Augmentation Method Sub-branches**: - Augmentation Stage - Pre-training - Fine-tuning - Inference - Augmentation Data - Unstructured Data - Structured Data - LLM Generated Content - Augmentation Process - Once Retrieval - Iterative Retrieval - Adaptive Retrieval 4. **Citations and References**: - **Better Semantic Representation**: - Chunk Optimization: Small2big; Sliding-window; Abstract-Embedding; Metadata Filtering [Liu] - Fine-tuning Embedding Model: PROMPTAGATOR [Dai et al., 2022]; BGE [BAAI, 2023]; LLM-Embedder [Zhang et al., 2023] - **Align Queries and Documents**: - Query Rewrite/Clarification: Query2Doc [Wang et al., 2023d]; RRPR [Ma et al., 2023a]; STEP-BACKPROMPTING [Zheng et al., 2023]; HyDE [Gao et al., 2022]; TROC [Kim et al., 2023] - **Align Retriever and LLM**: - Plugin Adapter: PKG [Luo et al., 2023]; RECOMP [Xu et al., 2023]; TokenUnifier [Berchansky et al., 2023] - LLM Supervised Training: AARV [Yang et al., 2023]; REPLUG [Shi et al., 2023]; Atlas [Izacard et al., 2022] - **Post-retrieval with Frozen LLM**: - Information Compression: PRCA [Yang et al., 2023]; RECOMP [Xu et al., 2023]; UPRISE [Cheng et al., 2023a] - Rerank: Reranker [Birger, 2023]; QLM [Zhang et al., 2023b] - General Optimization Process: Self-Mem [Cheng et al., 2023b] - Utilizing Contrastive Learning: SUGRE [Kang et al., 2023]; SANTAL [Li et al., 2023b] - **Fine-tuning LLM for RAG**: - RETRO [Borgeaud et al., 2022]; Atlas [Izacard et al., 2022]; REALM [Arora et al., 2021]; Toolformer [Schick et al., 2023]; CRQA [Waze et al., 2021]; RePrompt [Huang et al., 2023]; RETRO++ [Wang et al., 2023b]; InstructRetro [Wang et al., 2023c] - **Augmentation Stage**: - DPR [Karpukhin et al., 2020]; UPRISE [Cheng et al., 2023a]; Self-RAG [Asai et al., 2022]; DR-TA [Lin et al., 2023]; Self-TA [Aghaj et al., 2023]; SANTAL [Li et al., 2023b]; REPLUG [Shi et al., 2023]; AARV [Yang et al., 2023] - **Augmentation Data**: - KNN-LM [Khandelwal et al., 2019]; DSP [Khatta et al., 2023]; RAG [Lewis et al., 2020]; PRCA [Yang et al., 2023]; RLCOT [Huang et al., 2023]; GenRead [Yu et al., 2023]; ICRALM [Tang et al., 2023] - **Augmentation Process**: - UPRISE [Cheng et al., 2023a]; CREAI-LL [Li et al.,

1. **Type of Visual**: Architecture diagram 2. **Title or Heading**: Not explicitly titled, but the diagram is divided into three labeled sections: (a), (b), and (c). 3. **Axis Labels, Units, and Scale Ranges**: Not applicable. 4. **Data Series Names and Approximate Values**: Not applicable. 5. **Text Visible in the Image**: - **(a) Section**: - "Output" - "Hidden" - "Input" - "d = 4" - "d = 2" - "d = 1" - "O" - "H" - "H" - "I" - Variables: \( \hat{y}_0, \hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \ldots, \hat{y}_T \) - Variables: \( x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T \) - **(b) Section**: - "Residual block (k, d)" - "Dropout" - "ReLU" - "WeightNorm" - "Dilated Causal Conv" - "1x1 Conv (optional)" - Variables: \( \hat{z}^{(i)} = (z_0^{(i)}, \ldots, z_T^{(i)}) \) - Variables: \( \hat{z}^{(i-1)} = (z_0^{(i-1)}, \ldots, z_T^{(i-1)}) \) - **(c) Section**: - "Residual block (k=3, d=1)" - "Convolutional Filter" - "Identity Map (or 1x1 Conv)" - Variables: \( \hat{z}_{T-1}^{(1)}, \hat{z}_T^{(1)} \) - Variables: \( x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_T \) 6. **Specific Numbers, Percentages, Dates, Currency Values, or Named Entities**: - "k=3, d=1" in section (c). 7. **Overall Trend, Conclusion, or Key Insight**: - The diagram illustrates a neural network architecture with residual blocks. It shows the flow of data through input, hidden, and output layers, emphasizing the use of dilated causal convolutions and residual connections. 8. **Tables**: Not applicable. 9. **Diagram Components, Relationships, and Directional Flow**: - **(a) Section**: Shows a multi-layer architecture with input, hidden, and output layers. Data flows from the input layer through two hidden layers to the output layer, with dilation factors \( d = 1, 2, 4 \). - **(b) Section**: Details a residual block with components like Dropout, ReLU, WeightNorm, and Dilated Causal Convolution. It includes an optional 1x1 convolution and a residual connection. - **(c) Section**: Illustrates a specific residual block configuration with \( k=3 \) and \( d=1 \), showing the convolutional filter and identity map or 1x1 convolution. This detailed description provides a comprehensive understanding of the architecture and components depicted in the image.

📄 An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of visual**: Line graphs 2. **Title or heading**: Not explicitly titled, but the graphs are labeled as (a) \( T = 200 \) and (b) \( T = 600 \). 3. **Axis labels, units of measurement, and scale ranges**: - X-axis: "Iteration" ranging from 0 to 7000. - Y-axis: "Testing loss" ranging from 0.00 to 0.25. 4. **Data series names and approximate values**: - **TCN 7x27, k=6 (70K)** (blue line, graph a): Starts at ~0.20, drops sharply to ~0.05 by iteration 1000, stabilizes around 0.05. - **LSTM (70K)** (red line, both graphs): Starts at ~0.20, remains constant throughout at ~0.20. - **GRU (70K)** (cyan line, both graphs): Starts at ~0.20, drops sharply to ~0.05 by iteration 1000, stabilizes around 0.05. - **AWD-LSTM (70K)** (green line, both graphs): Starts at ~0.20, drops to ~0.10 by iteration 3000, further decreases to ~0.05 by iteration 5000, stabilizes around 0.05. 5. **Text visible in the image**: - Legend: "TCN 7x27, k=6 (70K)", "LSTM (70K)", "GRU (70K)", "AWD-LSTM (70K)" - Captions: "(a) \( T = 200 \)", "(b) \( T = 600 \)" 6. **Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities**: - Iterations range from 0 to 7000. - Testing loss values range from 0.00 to 0.25. 7. **Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight**: - In both graphs, TCN and GRU models show a rapid decrease in testing loss, stabilizing at a lower loss compared to LSTM, which remains constant. AWD-LSTM shows a gradual decrease, eventually stabilizing at a similar low loss as TCN and GRU. 8. **For tables**: Not applicable. 9. **For diagrams**: Not applicable.

📄 An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling.pdf
LLaVA Description:
The image contains two line graphs labeled as (a) and (b), each depicting "Testing Loss" over "Iteration" for different neural network models. The graphs compare the performance of various models on a task with different sequence lengths, \( T = 500 \) and \( T = 1000 \). ### Graph (a): \( T = 500 \) 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "Iteration" ranging from 0 to 3000 - Y-axis: "Testing Loss" ranging from 0.00 to 0.08 3. **Data Series**: - **TCN 9x10, k=6 (10K)**: Blue line, starts at ~0.07, decreases sharply to ~0.01 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.01. - **GRU (16K)**: Red line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **LSTM (16K)**: Pink line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **EURNN (16K)**: Green line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **Guess 0 for all**: Black dashed line, constant at ~0.02. 4. **Text**: - Caption: "(a) \( T = 500 \)" ### Graph (b): \( T = 1000 \) 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "Iteration" ranging from 0 to 3000 - Y-axis: "Testing Loss" ranging from 0.00 to 0.08 3. **Data Series**: - **TCN 8x10, k=8 (13K)**: Blue line, starts at ~0.07, decreases sharply to ~0.005 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.005. - **GRU (16K)**: Red line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **LSTM (16K)**: Pink line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **EURNN (16K)**: Green line, starts at ~0.08, decreases to ~0.02 by iteration 500, stabilizes around 0.02. - **Guess 0 for all**: Black dashed line, constant at ~0.02. 4. **Text**: - Caption: "(b) \( T = 1000 \)" ### Overall Trends and Insights: - The TCN models (both 9x10 and 8x10 configurations) show a significantly faster and lower convergence in testing loss compared to GRU, LSTM, and EURNN models for both sequence lengths. - The "Guess 0 for all" baseline remains constant at ~0.02, indicating the performance level of a naive guessing strategy. - The TCN models outperform other models in terms of achieving lower testing loss, especially noticeable in graph (b) with \( T = 1000 \). ### Legend: - **TCN**: Temporal Convolutional Network - **GRU**: Gated Recurrent Unit - **LSTM**: Long Short-Term Memory - **EURNN**: Efficient Unitary Recurrent Neural Network

📄 An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling.pdf
LLaVA Description:
This image contains two line graphs comparing the testing accuracy of different neural network models on two datasets: Sequential MNIST and P-MNIST. 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graphs 2. **Titles/Headings**: - (a) Sequential MNIST - (b) P-MNIST 3. **Axis Labels, Units, and Scale Ranges**: - X-axis: "Iteration" ranging from 0 to 8000 - Y-axis: "Testing accuracy" ranging from 0.0 to 1.0 4. **Data Series Names and Approximate Values**: - **TCN 8x25, k=7 (66K)**: - Sequential MNIST: Starts around 0.2, quickly rises to above 0.9 by iteration 1000, and stabilizes close to 1.0. - P-MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to above 0.9 by iteration 1000, and stabilizes close to 1.0. - **TCN 8x20, k=6 (41K)**: - Sequential MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.8 by iteration 1000, and stabilizes around 0.9. - P-MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.8 by iteration 1000, and stabilizes around 0.9. - **LSTM (68K)**: - Sequential MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.7 by iteration 1000, with a dip around iteration 4000, stabilizing around 0.8. - P-MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.7 by iteration 1000, stabilizing around 0.8. - **GRU (68K)**: - Sequential MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.8 by iteration 1000, with a dip around iteration 4000, stabilizing around 0.85. - P-MNIST: Starts around 0.2, rises to about 0.8 by iteration 1000, stabilizing around 0.85. 5. **Text Visible in the Image**: - Legend: - Blue: TCN 8x25, k=7 (66K) - Yellow: TCN 8x20, k=6 (41K) - Red: LSTM (68K) - Cyan: GRU (68K) - Captions: - (a) Sequential MNIST - (b) P-MNIST 6. **Specific Numbers and Named Entities**: - TCN 8x25, k=7 (66K) - TCN 8x20, k=6 (41K) - LSTM (68K) - GRU (68K) 7. **Overall Trend/Conclusion**: - The TCN models (both configurations) achieve higher testing accuracy more quickly and stabilize at higher accuracy levels compared to LSTM and GRU models on both datasets. The TCN 8x25, k=7 model performs the best, reaching near-perfect accuracy. This detailed description provides a comprehensive overview of the image, allowing for precise document retrieval based on the visual content.

📄 An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. **Type of visual**: Line graph 2. **Title or heading**: Not specified in the image 3. **Axis labels, units of measurement, and scale ranges**: - X-axis: "Sequence length T" with a range from 0 to 250 - Y-axis: "Testing Accuracy" with a range from 0 to 100 4. **Data series names and approximate values**: - **TCN (10K)**: Represented by a blue line. Maintains a constant testing accuracy of 100% across all sequence lengths from 0 to 250. - **LSTM (10K)**: Represented by a red line. Starts at approximately 80% accuracy at sequence length 0, decreases sharply to around 20% by sequence length 50, and then gradually declines to just above 10% by sequence length 250. - **GRU (10K)**: Represented by a cyan line. Begins at approximately 80% accuracy at sequence length 0, decreases to around 30% by sequence length 50, and then gradually declines to just above 10% by sequence length 250. - **Random Guess**: Represented by a black dashed line. Maintains a constant testing accuracy of approximately 10% across all sequence lengths. 5. **Text visible in the image**: - Legend: "TCN (10K)", "LSTM (10K)", "GRU (10K)", "Random Guess" 6. **Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities**: - TCN maintains 100% accuracy - LSTM and GRU start at approximately 80% accuracy - Random Guess is at approximately 10% accuracy 7. **Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight**: - TCN consistently achieves 100% testing accuracy regardless of sequence length. - LSTM and GRU both start with high accuracy but decline significantly as sequence length increases, eventually approaching the accuracy of a random guess. - The graph suggests that TCN is more robust to increasing sequence lengths compared to LSTM and GRU. 8. **For tables**: Not applicable 9. **For diagrams**: Not applicable

📄 An Empirical Evaluation of Generic Convolutional and Recurrent Networks for Sequence Modeling.pdf
LLaVA Description:
The image contains six line graphs, each with specific titles, axis labels, legends, and data series. Here is a detailed description: 1. **Graph (a): Different k on Copy Memory Task** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 3000) - **Y-axis:** Testing Loss (range: 0.00 to 0.06) - **Data Series:** - TCN with k=2: Starts at ~0.06, decreases rapidly to ~0.00 by iteration 500. - TCN with k=3: Similar trend as k=2. - TCN with k=4: Similar trend as k=2. - TCN with k=6: Starts at ~0.06, decreases to ~0.01 by iteration 2500. - TCN with k=8: Similar trend as k=6. - **Annotation:** "Guess 0 for all" as a dashed line at ~0.02. 2. **Graph (b): Different k on P-MNIST** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 6000) - **Y-axis:** Testing accuracy (range: 0.70 to 1.00) - **Data Series:** - TCN k=2 (67K): Starts at ~0.70, increases to ~0.95. - TCN k=3 (67K): Similar trend as k=2. - TCN k=4 (67K): Similar trend as k=2. - TCN k=6 (67K): Similar trend as k=2. - TCN k=8 (67K): Similar trend as k=2. 3. **Graph (c): Different k on PTB (word)** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 90) - **Y-axis:** Testing perplexity (range: 90 to 140) - **Data Series:** - TCN k=2 (13M): Starts at ~140, decreases to ~90. - TCN k=3 (13M): Similar trend as k=2. - TCN k=4 (13M): Similar trend as k=2. 4. **Graph (d): Residual on Copy Memory Task** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 3000) - **Y-axis:** Testing Loss (range: 0.00 to 0.03) - **Data Series:** - TCN w/ residual (13K): Starts at ~0.03, decreases to ~0.00 by iteration 500. - TCN w/o residual (13K): Similar trend as w/ residual. - **Annotation:** "Guess 0 for all" as a dashed line at ~0.02. 5. **Graph (e): Residual on P-MNIST** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 6000) - **Y-axis:** Testing accuracy (range: 0.70 to 1.00) - **Data Series:** - TCN w/ residual (67K): Starts at ~0.70, increases to ~0.95. - TCN w/o residual (67K): Similar trend as w/ residual. - TCN w/o residual (10K): Starts at ~0.70, increases to ~0.90. - TCN w/o residual (10K): Similar trend as w/o residual (10K). 6. **Graph (f): Residual on PTB (word)** - **Type:** Line graph - **X-axis:** Iteration (range: 0 to 90) - **Y-axis:** Testing perplexity (range: 90 to 140) - **Data Series:** - TCN w/ residual k=2 (13M): Starts at ~140, decreases to ~90. - TCN w/o residual k=2 (13M): Similar trend as w/ residual. - TCN w/ residual k=3 (13M): Similar trend as w/ residual. - TCN w/o residual k=3 (13M): Similar trend as w/ residual. **Overall Trends and Insights:** - Increasing k generally improves performance across tasks. - Residual connections tend to enhance model performance, reducing loss and perplexity while increasing accuracy. - The models converge quickly, with significant improvements in early iterations.

📄 MobileNetV2 Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks.pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. Type of visual: Architecture diagram 2. Title or heading visible in the image: "Separable Convolution Block" 3. Axis labels, units of measurement, and scale ranges: Not applicable 4. Data series name and approximate values: Not applicable 5. All text visible in the image: - "Separable Convolution Block" - "HxW" (label on the first block) - "1x1" (label on the second block) 6. Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities: - "HxW" indicates the height and width of the input feature map. - "1x1" indicates the size of the convolutional filter in the depthwise convolution. 7. Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight the visual communicates: - The diagram illustrates the concept of a separable convolution block, which involves a depthwise convolution followed by a pointwise convolution. This approach is used to reduce computational complexity by separating spatial and channel-wise operations. 8. For tables: Not applicable 9. For diagrams: - Components: - First block (left): Represents the input feature map with dimensions HxW. - Second block (middle): Represents the depthwise convolution operation with a filter size of 1x1. - Third block (right): Represents the output feature map after the separable convolution. - Relationships: The input feature map undergoes a depthwise convolution followed by a pointwise convolution to produce the output feature map. - Directional flow: From left to right, starting with the input feature map, through the depthwise convolution, and ending with the output feature map.

Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities: - "3x3" refers to the size of a convolutional filter. - "1x1" refers to the size of another convolutional filter. 7. Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight: The diagram illustrates the concept of a bottleneck convolution, which involves reducing the dimensionality of data using a 1x1 convolution, applying a dense 3x3 convolution, and then reducing again with another 1x1 convolution. 8. Table column headers and summary of row data: Not applicable 9. Diagram components, their relationships, and directional flow: - The diagram consists of four main components, represented as 3D blocks. - The first block is labeled "3x3" and represents the initial input. - The second block is labeled "Reduce, Dense" and shows the application of a dense 3x3 convolution. - The third block is labeled "Reduce, 1x1" and represents the application of a 1x1 convolution to reduce dimensionality. - The fourth block is a larger, semi-transparent block indicating the output after the bottleneck process. - The flow is from left to right, showing the transformation of data through the bottleneck convolution process.

The "Reduction Cell" appears to have a more complex structure with additional operations compared to the "Normal Cell." 8. **Tables**: Not applicable. 9. **Components, Relationships, and Directional Flow**: - **Normal Cell**: - Inputs: "h", "h", "s_{n-1}", "s_n" - Operations: "add", "sep 5x5", "sep 3x3", "identity" - Output: "concat" - Flow: Inputs are processed through various operations and combined at the "concat" node. - **Reduction Cell**: - Inputs: "h", "h", "s_{n-1}", "s_n" - Operations: "add", "sep 5x5", "sep 3x3", "identity" - Output: "concat" - Flow: Similar to the Normal Cell but with a more intricate arrangement of operations leading to the "concat" node. 📄 MobileNetV2 Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks.pdf
LLaVA Description:
The image consists of two line graphs side by side, each illustrating different aspects of neural network performance. ### Left Graph: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Title/Heading**: Not specified 3. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "Exp. ratios" with a scale from 0 to 7 - Y-axis: "Top-1 accuracy" with a scale from 65 to 72 4. **Data Series**: - "Linear bottleneck" (blue line): Starts at approximately 66.5 accuracy at an exp. ratio of 0, rising to about 71.5 at an exp. ratio of 7. - "Relu6 in bottleneck" (green line): Starts at approximately 66 accuracy at an exp. ratio of 0, rising to about 70 at an exp. ratio of 7. 5. **Text**: - Legend: "Linear bottleneck" (blue), "Relu6 in bottleneck" (green) ### Right Graph: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Title/Heading**: Not specified 3. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "Exp. ratios" with a scale from 0 to 7 - Y-axis: "Top-1 accuracy" with a scale from 65 to 72 4. **Data Series**: - "Shortcut between bottlenecks" (blue line): Starts at approximately 66.5 accuracy at an exp. ratio of 0, rising to about 71.5 at an exp. ratio of 7. - "Shortcut between expansions" (green line): Starts at approximately 66 accuracy at an exp. ratio of 0, rising to about 70 at an exp. ratio of 7. - "No residual" (red line): Starts at approximately 66 accuracy at an exp. ratio of 0, rising to about 69 at an exp. ratio of 7. 5. **Text**: - Legend: "Shortcut between bottlenecks" (blue), "Shortcut between expansions" (green), "No residual" (red) ### Overall Trend/Insight: - The graphs compare different configurations of neural network architectures in terms of their top-1 accuracy as a function of expansion ratios. - In both graphs, the blue lines ("Linear bottleneck" and "Shortcut between bottlenecks") consistently show higher accuracy across all expansion ratios compared to the other configurations. - The use of shortcuts and linear bottlenecks appears to improve accuracy compared to using ReLU6 or having no residual connections.

LLaVA Description:
The image contains two sets of line graphs, labeled (a) and (b), each with two subplots. These graphs analyze the behavior of filters in a neural network across different layers. ### (a) At step 0 #### Left Subplot: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **X-axis**: "Layer N" ranging from 0 to 16 3. **Y-axis**: "Num positive filters" ranging from 0 to 1000 4. **Data Series**: - **avg**: Starts around 100 at Layer 0, increasing to about 400 at Layer 15 - **min**: Starts near 0, rising to about 200 at Layer 15 - **max**: Starts around 200, increasing to about 1000 at Layer 15 - **total**: Starts around 100, increasing to about 800 at Layer 15 - **threshold**: Constant at approximately 100 5. **Legend**: Located in the top left corner #### Right Subplot: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **X-axis**: "Layer N" ranging from 0 to 16 3. **Y-axis**: "Fraction of filters" ranging from 0 to 1 4. **Data Series**: - **avg**: Starts around 0.2, increasing to about 0.5 at Layer 15 - **min**: Starts near 0, rising to about 0.2 at Layer 15 - **max**: Starts around 0.4, increasing to about 0.8 at Layer 15 - **total**: Starts around 0.2, increasing to about 0.6 at Layer 15 - **threshold**: Constant at approximately 0.1 5. **Shaded Area**: Represents variability around the average ### (b) Fully trained #### Left Subplot: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **X-axis**: "Layer N" ranging from 0 to 16 3. **Y-axis**: "Num positive filters" ranging from 0 to 1000 4. **Data Series**: - **avg**: Starts around 100 at Layer 0, increasing to about 600 at Layer 15 - **min**: Starts near 0, rising to about 300 at Layer 15 - **max**: Starts around 200, increasing to about 1000 at Layer 15 - **total**: Starts around 100, increasing to about 900 at Layer 15 - **threshold**: Constant at approximately 100 5. **Legend**: Located in the top left corner #### Right Subplot: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **X-axis**: "Layer N" ranging from 0 to 16 3. **Y-axis**: "Fraction of filters" ranging from 0 to 1 4. **Data Series**: - **avg**: Starts around 0.2, increasing to about 0.6 at Layer 15 - **min**: Starts near 0, rising to about 0.3 at Layer 15 - **max**: Starts around 0.4, increasing to about 0.9 at Layer 15 - **total**: Starts around 0.2, increasing to about 0.7 at Layer 15 - **threshold**: Constant at approximately 0.1 5. **Shaded Area**: Represents variability around the average ### Overall Trends and Insights: - The number of positive filters and the fraction of filters generally increase with layer depth in both initial and fully trained states. - The variability (shaded area) around the average is more pronounced in the fully trained state. - The maximum values show a significant increase, especially in deeper layers, indicating more filters become positive as training progresses.

📄 ResNet Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (He et al 2016).pdf
LLaVA Description:
The image consists of two line graphs side by side, comparing the performance of neural networks with different layer depths. **Left Graph: Training Error** 1. **Type of Visual:** Line graph 2. **Title/Heading:** Not specified 3. **Axis Labels:** - X-axis: "iter. (1e4)" with a scale range from 0 to 6.5 - Y-axis: "training error (%)" with a scale range from 0 to 20 4. **Data Series:** - "56-layer" (red line): Starts at approximately 20% training error, decreases to around 6% by the end of the iterations. - "20-layer" (yellow line): Starts at approximately 15% training error, decreases to around 3% by the end of the iterations. 5. **Visible Text:** "56-layer", "20-layer" 6. **Specific Numbers/Percentages:** Training error starts at 20% for 56-layer and 15% for 20-layer. 7. **Overall Trend/Insight:** The 20-layer network consistently achieves lower training error than the 56-layer network throughout the iterations. **Right Graph: Test Error** 1. **Type of Visual:** Line graph 2. **Title/Heading:** Not specified 3. **Axis Labels:** - X-axis: "iter. (1e4)" with a scale range from 0 to 6.5 - Y-axis: "test error (%)" with a scale range from 10 to 20 4. **Data Series:** - "56-layer" (red line): Starts at approximately 20% test error, decreases to around 10% by the end of the iterations. - "20-layer" (yellow line): Starts at approximately 18% test error, decreases to around 8% by the end of the iterations. 5. **Visible Text:** "56-layer", "20-layer" 6. **Specific Numbers/Percentages:** Test error starts at 20% for 56-layer and 18% for 20-layer. 7. **Overall Trend/Insight:** The 20-layer network consistently achieves lower test error than the 56-layer network throughout the iterations. **Key Insight:** The 20-layer network performs better in both training and test error compared to the 56-layer network, indicating that deeper networks do not necessarily lead to better performance in this context.

📄 ResNet Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (He et al 2016).pdf
LLaVA Description:
1. Type of visual: Architecture diagram 2. Title or heading: None visible in the image 3. Axis labels, units of measurement, and scale ranges: Not applicable 4. Data series name and approximate values: Not applicable 5. All text visible in the image: - "x" - "weight layer" - "relu" - "identity" - "F(x)" - "F(x) + x" 6. Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities: None 7. Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight: The diagram illustrates a residual block used in neural network architectures, specifically showing the flow of data through two weight layers with ReLU activations, and the addition of the input "x" to the output of these layers to form "F(x) + x". This is a key component of residual networks (ResNets) which helps in training deep networks by allowing gradients to flow through the identity shortcut connection. 8. Tables: Not applicable 9. Diagram components, their relationships, and directional flow: - Input "x" flows into the first "weight layer". - The output of the first "weight layer" passes through a "relu" activation function. - The result flows into the second "weight layer". - The output of the second "weight layer" is added to the original input "x" (identity connection) to form "F(x) + x". - The sum "F(x) + x" passes through another "relu" activation function. - The diagram highlights the identity shortcut connection, which bypasses the weight layers and directly adds the input "x" to the output of the second weight layer.
cc382fd4-b5d6-43f0-a6c6-2b87b3322ac0
📄 ResNet Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (He et al 2016).pdf
LLaVA Description:
This image consists of two line graphs side by side, comparing error rates over iterations for different neural network architectures. ### Left Graph: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Title/Heading**: Not explicitly titled 3. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "iter. (1e4)" with a scale from 0 to 60 - Y-axis: "error (%)" with a scale from 20% to 60% 4. **Data Series Names and Values**: - "plain-18" (cyan line): Starts at approximately 60% error, decreases to around 30% by iteration 60,000. - "plain-34" (red line): Starts at approximately 60% error, decreases to around 35% by iteration 60,000. 5. **Text**: - Legend: "plain-18" and "plain-34" - Annotations: "18-layer" in cyan, "34-layer" in red 6. **Specific Numbers**: Iterations range from 0 to 60,000; error rates range from 20% to 60%. 7. **Overall Trend**: Both networks show a decrease in error rate over iterations, with the 18-layer network performing slightly better than the 34-layer network. ### Right Graph: 1. **Type of Visual**: Line graph 2. **Title/Heading**: Not explicitly titled 3. **Axis Labels**: - X-axis: "iter. (1e4)" with a scale from 0 to 60 - Y-axis: "error (%)" with a scale from 20% to 60% 4. **Data Series Names and Values**: - "ResNet-18" (cyan line): Starts at approximately 60% error, decreases to around 30% by iteration 60,000. - "ResNet-34" (red line): Starts at approximately 60% error, decreases to around 25% by iteration 60,000. 5. **Text**: - Legend: "ResNet-18" and "ResNet-34" - Annotations: "18-layer" in cyan, "34-layer" in red 6. **Specific Numbers**: Iterations range from 0 to 60,000; error rates range from 20% to 60%. 7. **Overall Trend**: Both ResNet architectures show a significant decrease in error rate over iterations, with the 34-layer ResNet performing better than the 18-layer ResNet. ### Key Insight: The graphs compare plain networks and ResNet architectures, showing that ResNet architectures generally achieve lower error rates, with deeper ResNet models (34-layer) performing better than their shallower counterparts (18-layer).

Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities: - "64-d" and "256-d" indicating dimensions - "3x3, 64" and "1x1, 64" indicating kernel size and number of filters - "1x1, 256" indicating kernel size and number of filters 7. Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight: The diagrams represent two types of residual blocks used in neural network architectures. The left block is a basic residual block with two convolutional layers, while the right block is a bottleneck residual block with three convolutional layers, including two 1x1 convolutions for dimensionality reduction and expansion. 8. For tables: Not applicable 9. For diagrams: - Components: - Left diagram: Two "3x3, 64" convolutional layers with ReLU activations, a shortcut connection, and an addition operation followed by ReLU. - Right diagram: One "1x1, 64" convolutional layer, one "3x3, 64" convolutional layer, one "1x1, 256" convolutional layer, each followed by ReLU activations, a shortcut connection, and an addition operation followed by ReLU. - Relationships and directional flow: - Both diagrams show a flow from top to bottom, with input dimensions indicated at the top. - Convolutional layers are connected sequentially with ReLU activations. - A shortcut connection bypasses the convolutional layers and merges with the output through an addition operation, followed by a final ReLU activation.

X-axis: "epochs (1e2)" ranging from 0 to 7. - Y-axis: "error (%)" ranging from 0 to 20. 4. **Data series names and approximate values**: - **Left graph**: - "plain-20": Starts around 15%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - "plain-32": Starts around 18%, decreases to about 7% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 7%. - "plain-44": Starts around 19%, decreases to about 8% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 8%. - "plain-56": Starts around 20%, decreases to about 10% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 10%. - **Middle graph**: - "ResNet-20": Starts around 18%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - "ResNet-32": Starts around 19%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - "ResNet-44": Starts around 19%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - "ResNet-56": Starts around 20%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - "ResNet-110": Starts around 20%, decreases to about 5% by epoch 3, stabilizes around 5%. - **Right graph**: - "residual-110": Starts around 5%, remains stable around 5%. - "residual-1202": Starts around 10%, remains stable around 10%. 5. **Text visible in the image**: - Left graph legend: "plain-20", "plain-32", "plain-44", "plain-56". - Middle graph legend: "ResNet-20", "ResNet-32", "ResNet-44", "ResNet-56", "ResNet-110". - Right graph legend: "residual-110", "residual-1202". - Annotations: "20-layer", "56-layer", "110-layer", "1202-layer". 6. **Specific numbers, percentages, dates, currency values, or named entities**: - Error percentages range from 0% to 20%. - Epochs range from 0 to 7 (in units of 100). 7. **Overall trend, conclusion, or key insight**: - The plain networks (left graph) show higher error rates compared to ResNet architectures (middle graph). - ResNet architectures demonstrate a significant reduction in error rates, stabilizing around 5% after epoch 3. - The deeper ResNet models (110 and 1202 layers) maintain low error rates, with the 1202-layer model stabilizing around 10%. 8. **For tables**: Not applicable. 9. **For diagrams**: Not applicable.

The ResNet architectures (ResNet-20, ResNet-56, ResNet-110) show a more stable and consistent decrease in standard deviation compared to the plain architectures (plain-20, plain-56). The bottom graph, which sorts layers by magnitude, shows a more pronounced and smooth decrease in standard deviation across all architectures. 8. **For Tables**: Not applicable. 9. **For Diagrams**: Not applicable.
